sections.title: The Big Picture
paragraphs: The 12-team College Football Playoff sets the structure: four autobids for SEC, Big Ten, ACC, and Big 12 champions, with the remaining eight slots filled by résumé and rating. We simulated the full 2026 season 1,000 times — every game, every conference championship, every bracket — using ESPN FPI preseason ratings and a logistic win model calibrated on 2025 results. Team strength flows through FPI margins; home-field advantage is modeled separately at draw time rather than baked into the margin. No two runs produced the same playoff field.

That variance is the headline. Across 1,000 unique 12-team fields, Power Four leagues average 10.1 teams per bracket — the Big Ten (~3.6), SEC (~3.6), Big 12 (~1.6), and ACC (~1.4) supply most of the inventory. Notre Dame, without a conference autobid, appears in 87.5% of fields. Every playoff field includes exactly one Group of 6 autobid.

National title equity is concentrated in the Big Ten: the league accounts for roughly 47% of all championship odds, led by Ohio State. The SEC follows at about 28%. The model does not reward reputation on its own — it rewards rating, schedule, and the paths that survive a thousand different Novembers.

sections.title: The National Championship Race
paragraphs: Ohio State leads the title race at about 17% — the highest single-program share in the model, though far from a majority. Oregon (~11%), Indiana (~10%), and Notre Dame (~10%) form a crowded tier behind the Buckeyes. Georgia (~8%) is the SEC's top championship equity; Texas Tech (~5%) leads the Big 12.

The gap between making the playoff and winning it matters. Notre Dame reaches the field in 87.5% of sims but carries 10.3% title equity — a profile built on schedule and the 10-win independent rule. Ohio State, Oregon, and Indiana pair stronger title odds with top-tier playoff frequency. Texas Tech averages 10.94 wins and clears the playoff bar in 60.0% of runs.

Below the top tier, Miami (~5%), Texas (~5%), Alabama (~3%), and Penn State (~3%) remain live long shots. No program clears 18% title equity — this is a fragmented race where seeding, matchup luck, and a single upset can swing January outcomes.

featured_teams.caption: Big Ten favorite · 10.15 avg wins · 34.4% conf title

sections.title: The Playoff Landscape
paragraphs: Playoff frequency separates the locks from the hopefuls. Notre Dame clears the bar in 87.5% of simulations — the model's most reliable field presence. Ohio State (67.9%), Oregon (67.5%), Indiana (66.8%), and Georgia (64.2%) form the next tier. Texas Tech (60.0%), Miami (54.9%), and Penn State (46.3%) round out the programs that make the bracket in nearly half of all runs or better.

The most common co-occurrences in playoff fields run through South Bend. Ohio State Buckeyes and Notre Dame share a bracket in 59.0% of sims; Oregon Ducks–Notre Dame appears in 59.5%; Indiana Hoosiers–Notre Dame in 58.2%. The Irish are the gravitational center — contenders are often judged by whether they share a field with Notre Dame.

The bubble lives in the low twenties: Ole Miss (24.6%), USC (23.7%), Tennessee (23.6%), Oklahoma (23.1%), and Michigan (22.3%). Texas (43.5%) and Alabama (38.1%) sit higher than a typical bubble tier but still fight for at-large slots behind the top seeds. With 1,000 distinct fields, there is no single 'last team in' — only probabilities.

sections.title: What to Watch
paragraphs: Schedule strength drives variance. Texas faces the toughest opponent slate in the SEC (SOS +10.4 in the model); Ohio State (+6.8) and Michigan (+7.6) navigate a demanding Big Ten. Teams with soft schedules can pile up wins but still need rating to survive committee-style selection in the sims.

The Group of 6 autobid is guaranteed: every playoff field includes exactly one G6 team. Boise State (12.9% field rate) and Toledo (6.8%) are the most frequent bid winners. South Florida (6.8%) leads the American race; James Madison (2.5%) remains the Sun Belt's best win-total profile.

Watch the independents and the coin-flip games. Notre Dame's schedule is light on paper (SOS +0.2) but the Irish still reach the field more than anyone. Late-season conference matchups with win probabilities near 50% — especially in the SEC and Big Ten — are where playoff pictures fracture across the 1,000 runs.

The SEC supplies 28.0% of national title equity and averages 3.6 teams per playoff field — the deepest inventory of any league. Georgia leads the conference race at 25.1% conf title odds with 64.2% playoff frequency. Texas (15.4% conf title, 43.5% playoff) and Alabama (9.9%, 38.1%) chase the Bulldogs despite brutal schedules.

Texas carries the league's highest SOS (+10.4) — a gauntlet that caps win totals but keeps playoff equity alive. Oklahoma (23.1% playoff) is still climbing in year three of SEC membership. The conference autobid is rarely in doubt; the fight is for seeds, byes, and survival in November.

No league concentrates championship equity like the Big Ten: 46.6% of all national title odds across Ohio State (17.03%), Oregon (10.86%), Indiana (10.49%), and Penn State (2.63%). The conference places a member in every simulated playoff field and averages 3.6 teams per bracket.

Indiana is the breakout — 66.8% playoff frequency and 10.33 avg wins despite a light SOS (+1.7). Ohio State's path is harder (+6.8 SOS) but the Buckeyes remain the league favorite at 34.4% conference title odds. Purdue (3.05 avg wins) anchors the bottom; the middle is crowded with playoff fringe teams like Iowa and Michigan.

Miami dominates the ACC model: 46.9% conference title odds, 54.9% playoff frequency, and 5.05% national title equity — nearly half the league's championship share. The Hurricanes average 10.31 wins and win the ACC title game in nearly two-thirds of appearances.

Louisville (18.6% playoff), SMU (16.9%), and Clemson (11.5%) form a second tier with paths to the field but minimal title equity. The ACC averages 1.4 teams per playoff bracket — respectable volume for a league whose champion is often Miami or a challenger from the middle pack.

Texas Tech is the Big 12's flagship in this model: 40.3% conference title odds, 60.0% playoff frequency, 5.35% national title equity, and 10.94 avg wins. The Red Raiders benefit from a manageable SOS (-3.6) and win the league more often than any peer.

BYU (21.7% playoff) and Utah (18.0%) are the primary challengers. The league carries 7.9% of national title equity — competitive with the ACC — and averages 1.6 teams per field. Oklahoma State (4.45 avg wins) sits at the bottom of a league that rewards consistency over brand.

The American is a wide-open autobid race: Navy leads at 19.0% conference title odds, but South Florida (15.1%, 6.8% playoff) and East Carolina (13.9%) are co-favorites in practice. A league member appears in 35.7% of all playoff simulations.

South Florida averages 8.89 wins — the best record in the league — yet Navy's schedule profile keeps the Midshipmen atop the title odds. The American's path is almost entirely about winning the conference; national title equity across the league totals just 0.09%.

James Madison is the Sun Belt's strongest program by win totals: 40.6% conference title odds and 9.85 avg wins. The Dukes win the league in nearly a third of all simulations, though the G6 autobid path clears in only 2.5% of fields.

Old Dominion (23.2% conf title, 4.8% playoff) is the primary challenger. A Sun Belt team appears in 9.8% of playoff fields. The league's SOS (-11.5 for the Dukes) limits ceiling, but the autobid path is real and the win totals are not fluky.

Boise State leads the Pac-12 footprint at 40.7% conference title odds and 12.9% playoff frequency — the league's only program with meaningful national relevance at 0.07% title equity. The Broncos average 8.49 wins and dominate the conference championship game when they arrive.

Washington State (18.4%) and Texas State (12.1%) chase the autobid. A Pac-12 member appears in 34.0% of playoff fields. This is a survivor's league — programs without a Power Four home fighting for one bid and the legitimacy that comes with it.

Toledo leads the MAC at 31.9% conference title odds with 9.17 avg wins and 6.8% playoff field rate — the league's best autobid case. Miami (OH) (19.7%) and Ohio (17.2%) push the Rockets in a league where one loss can end the season's meaning.

MAC teams face the weakest SOS in FBS (Toledo at -14.9). A league member reaches the playoff field in 12.5% of simulations. The path is narrow — win the conference, accept a low seed, and hope the bracket breaks right.

UNLV tops the Mountain West at 27.9% conference title odds, but North Dakota State remains the league's win-total story: 8.94 avg wins and 25.3% conference title odds. The Bison are a perennial autobid candidate when the cross-league tiebreaks break their way.

A Mountain West member appears in 6.4% of playoff fields. Air Force (11.6%) and New Mexico (15.1%) add depth to a league where the autobid race is genuinely contested. Total national title equity is 0.01% — autobid or bust.

Conference USA is the thinnest autobid race in FBS: Western Kentucky leads at 22.8% title odds, Liberty follows at 19.9%, and no CUSA team carries measurable national championship equity. A league member reaches the playoff in just 1.6% of simulations.

Liberty (8.02 avg wins, 0.3% playoff) is the most likely bid winner if WKU falters. Jacksonville State (16.5%) adds a third contender. The league's SOS profiles (−9 to −15) make win totals the primary currency — and even 7–5 might not be enough.
